class_schedule.topic: The E-commerce Landscape and Business Models
learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of e-commerce from the 1995 launch of Amazon and eBay through today's trillion-dollar industry
Distinguish between the major e-commerce business models: B2C, B2B, C2C, D2C, marketplace, and SaaS
Explain the eight unique features of e-commerce technology and their business implications
Identify the key revenue models: sales, subscription, advertising, affiliate, transaction fee, and freemium
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024: Business, Technology, Society (18th ed.), Ch. 1 - The Revolution Is Just Beginning
Chaffey, Digital Business and E-commerce Management (7th ed.), Ch. 1 - Introduction to Digital Business and E-commerce
Harvard Business Review, 'Strategy and the Internet' by Michael Porter (2001) - foundational framework article

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1994, Jeff Bezos left his hedge fund job and drove to Seattle to start an online bookstore. In 1995, Pierre Omidyar posted a broken laser pointer for sale on his personal website, sold it for $14.83, and launched what became eBay. Thirty years later, e-commerce generates over $6 trillion in global retail sales, Amazon is worth more than the next ten largest retailers combined, and Shopify powers over 4 million merchants representing 10% of all U.S. e-commerce. Yet the failure rate remains brutal: Pets.com, Webvan, Kozmo, and eToys burned through $2 billion in the dot-com era, and recent casualties include Jet.com, Boxed, and dozens of venture-backed D2C brands that scaled faster than their unit economics could support. Understanding why some e-commerce businesses thrive while others collapse requires more than technology knowledge; it requires understanding business models, unit economics, network effects, and the unique dynamics of digital markets. This course takes you from the foundations of e-commerce business models through platforms, payments, logistics, marketing, personalization, international expansion, and emerging formats like social commerce and livestream shopping. You will learn to analyze real companies, calculate the economics that make or break them, and build the judgment to recognize sustainable digital businesses from hype-driven failures.
key_concepts: Eight unique features of e-commerce (Laudon & Traver): ubiquity, global reach, universal standards, richness, interactivity, information density, personalization/customization, and social technology
B2C (Business-to-Consumer): Amazon retail, Warby Parker, Zappos selling directly to end consumers
B2B (Business-to-Business): Alibaba.com, Grainger, ThomasNet selling between businesses, over 6x the revenue of B2C
C2C (Consumer-to-Consumer): eBay, Etsy, Poshmark, Depop, Facebook Marketplace, typically monetized via listing or transaction fees
D2C (Direct-to-Consumer): Warby Parker, Casper, Allbirds, Dollar Shave Club manufacturer-branded sales bypassing retail
Marketplace model: Amazon Marketplace, Etsy, Airbnb, Uber taking 10-30% commission, capital-light but requires solving chicken-and-egg problem
SaaS: Shopify, Salesforce, Slack subscription software with 70-85% gross margins valued on ARR and net revenue retention
Revenue models: sales, subscription, advertising, affiliate, transaction fee, freemium each with different economics
Network effects: two-sided (eBay), data (Amazon recommendations), social (Instagram shopping) create defensibility
Porter framework reinterpreted: internet lowers barriers, increases rivalry, shifts power to buyers, forcing defensible advantages through brand, scale, data, or network effects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Open Amazon.com, Shopify.com, eBay.com, Warby Parker, and Alibaba.com in tabs. For each, identify: the business model category, primary revenue source, who pays whom, and what creates the competitive moat. Then pull up the Amazon 10-K to see how it segments revenue: Online Stores 39%, Third-party Seller Services 24%, AWS 16%, Subscription 7%, Advertising 8%, Physical Stores 4%. Discuss how this real segmentation reveals Amazon's actual business model vs the surface 'online retailer' impression.
setup_requirements: Web browser with tabs for major e-commerce sites (Amazon, Shopify, eBay, Alibaba, Warby Parker, Etsy)
Access to SEC EDGAR for 10-K filings: www.sec.gov/edgar
Textbook: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024 (18th ed.)
Class Slack channel for real-time discussion of cases

review.value: A business model describes WHO you serve and HOW you create value; a revenue model describes HOW you capture that value as money. Amazon's B2C business model is paired with multiple revenue models (retail, Prime, ads, marketplace) which is why it is durable

Bezos's napkin sketch shows the self-reinforcing loop: lower prices attract customers, which attracts sellers, which increases selection, which attracts customers. This creates a network effect that has proven nearly impossible to dislodge after 30 years

D2C was marketed as a way to capture retailer margin, but most D2C brands found CAC on Facebook and Google consumed more than the saved margin. Casper went public at $14.50 in 2020 and was taken private at $6.90 in 2022 - a cautionary tale

Every marketplace must solve the cold-start problem: sellers won't join without buyers, buyers won't come without selection. Airbnb manually signed up NYC hosts. eBay started with collectibles. Unsolved chicken-and-egg kills most marketplace startups

learning_objectives: Compare major e-commerce platforms (Shopify, WooCommerce, Magento/Adobe Commerce, BigCommerce, Salesforce Commerce Cloud) on features, cost, and target segment
Explain the role of headless commerce architecture and when to choose it over monolithic platforms
Evaluate the trade-offs between marketplace selling (Amazon, Etsy, Walmart.com) and owned storefronts
Map the technical stack of a modern e-commerce site: front-end, back-end, database, CDN, search, payments, email, analytics
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 4 - Building an E-commerce Presence
Chaffey, Digital Business and E-commerce Management (7th ed.), Ch. 3 - Managing Digital Business Infrastructure
Shopify Engineering Blog: 'How Shopify Scaled Its Database Layer' (2022)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2004, building an online store meant hiring developers to write custom PHP code, setting up servers, integrating a payment gateway, and budgeting $50,000 to $500,000 before your first sale. Today, a teenager can launch a fully functional store in an afternoon for $39/month using Shopify, with free themes, one-click payment setup, and built-in shipping labels. This democratization of e-commerce infrastructure is the single most important shift in digital business since Amazon itself. Tobi Lutke started Shopify in 2006 after trying to sell snowboards online and finding existing platforms inadequate; the company now processes over $235 billion in annual gross merchandise volume and supports 10% of all U.S. e-commerce. But Shopify is just one option. WooCommerce powers roughly 25% of online stores on top of WordPress. Magento/Adobe Commerce targets enterprise brands needing deep customization. Amazon, Walmart.com, and Etsy offer marketplace access without building a standalone site. And at the cutting edge, headless commerce architectures decouple the front-end from the back-end, allowing a Nike.com redesign without touching the commerce engine. This session teaches you to evaluate platforms not as a checklist of features but as a strategic decision: what fits your stage, your product, your technical capability, and your growth trajectory.
key_concepts: Shopify: hosted SaaS platform, transaction fees 0.5-2% in addition to payment processor, Shopify Plus for enterprise $2,000+/month; powers Kylie Cosmetics, Gymshark, Allbirds, Red Bull
WooCommerce: open-source WordPress plugin, self-hosted, free core with paid extensions; lowest cost but requires technical maintenance
Magento/Adobe Commerce: enterprise open-source (Adobe-owned since 2018), highly customizable, used by Ford, Coca-Cola, Nestle; implementation costs $100K-$2M
BigCommerce: SaaS alternative to Shopify with no transaction fees and stronger B2B features; used by Skullcandy and Ben & Jerry's
Salesforce Commerce Cloud: enterprise, revenue-share pricing 1-2% of GMV, used by Adidas, Burton, L'Oreal
Headless commerce: separates storefront (React, Next.js) from commerce backend via APIs; enables custom experiences but requires more engineering
Marketplace selling: Amazon Seller Central/Vendor Central, Walmart.com Marketplace, Etsy, eBay; access to traffic but platform dependence
Technical stack layers: front-end (theme/React), back-end (platform), database, CDN (Cloudflare, Fastly), search (Algolia, Elasticsearch), payments (Stripe), email (Klaviyo), analytics (GA4)
Total cost of ownership: platform fees + transaction fees + payment processing + apps + developer costs + migration, often 2-3x headline platform price
Platform lock-in risk: proprietary data structures, non-portable code (Shopify Liquid, Magento PHP), integration chains that make switching painful
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a Shopify store setup end-to-end in 15 minutes. Create a development store, pick a theme (Dawn), add three products with images and variants, configure Shopify Payments, enable USPS shipping rates, and place a test order. Then open the admin to show orders, inventory, customers, analytics, apps. Contrast with WooCommerce by showing a WordPress dashboard with WooCommerce installed, highlighting the plugin ecosystem. Finally, show a live headless example: Allbirds.com front-end is custom Next.js while their backend commerce is Shopify Plus accessed via Storefront API.
setup_requirements: Shopify free development store account (partners.shopify.com)
WordPress + WooCommerce demo site or screencast
Browser for inspecting real commerce sites
Spreadsheet for TCO comparison exercise

review.value: Platform choice determines what you can build and how fast you can move for years. A $50M DTC brand that started on WooCommerce often regrets not using Shopify Plus when they try to integrate ERP, subscription, and wholesale all at once

Shopify's real product is not just the storefront - it is the ecosystem of 13,000+ apps, 100+ payment gateways, built-in tax calculation, fulfillment network, and capital lending. This is why it commands higher prices than commodity hosting

Headless commerce gives design flexibility and performance at the cost of engineering complexity. A small brand does NOT need headless; it needs focus on product. Nike.com needs headless because Nike has 200 developers and custom UX requirements that exceed any template

Brands that sell only on Amazon are not building a business; they are building a channel. Amazon can suspend accounts overnight, change algorithm ranking, launch a private-label competitor, or raise FBA fees - each an existential risk an owned storefront does not have

learning_objectives: Map the payment flow from customer card tap to merchant bank account, identifying each intermediary and fee
Compare payment processors (Stripe, PayPal, Adyen, Square) on developer experience, fees, and international support
Evaluate buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) options (Affirm, Klarna, Afterpay) and their impact on conversion and basket size
Explain the fraud and chargeback ecosystem and the cost of both fraud attacks and false declines
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 5 - E-commerce Security and Payment Systems
Stripe Documentation: Payments, Radar (fraud), and Billing
Matt Levine, 'Money Stuff: The Interchange Economy' columns on credit card fees
Federal Reserve, 'The 2022 Federal Reserve Payments Study'

lecture_notes.introduction: When you tap a credit card on Amazon, you probably assume the money moves from your bank to Amazon. It does not. What actually happens is a dance among at least five parties - issuing bank, card network, acquiring bank, payment processor, merchant - each taking a cut before Amazon sees any funds, and none of it happens in real time. Amazon is typically paid two business days later. The Durbin-Kaufman hearings in 2010 revealed that U.S. merchants pay over $100 billion annually in interchange fees; this is a bigger transfer than most countries' GDP. Stripe was founded in 2010 by brothers Patrick and John Collison with a radical premise: developers should be able to accept payments with seven lines of code instead of a six-week merchant account application. Stripe is now worth $70B+ and processes over $1 trillion annually. PayPal, founded in 1998 by Max Levchin and Peter Thiel, dominates a different segment by riding on consumer trust. Klarna, Affirm, and Afterpay exploded BNPL in the 2020-2022 period, with Affirm going public at $49/share, rising to $176, and crashing back to $11 as rising interest rates exposed weak unit economics. This session unpacks the entire payment stack: who touches your money, what they charge, how fraud works, and how decisions in this layer can move conversion rates by 2-5%.
key_concepts: Interchange fees: the fee the acquiring bank pays the issuing bank, typically 1.5-3.5% + $0.10; set by Visa/Mastercard and passed through to the merchant
Assessment fees: Visa and Mastercard take ~0.13-0.15% on top of interchange
Processor markup: Stripe, Square, and Adyen charge 2.9% + $0.30 U.S. standard, bundling interchange + assessment + ~0.3-0.5% margin
Payment flow: Customer -> Shopify/Stripe checkout -> Processor -> Card network -> Issuing bank (authorization) -> Response -> Later settlement
PayPal: consumer wallet + processor hybrid; 3.49% + $0.49 standard; delivers conversion lift from saved credentials; Venmo integration drives younger users
Stripe's developer-first strategy: API-first, Radar fraud ML, Billing for subscriptions, Connect for marketplaces, Issuing for virtual cards
Adyen: preferred for global merchants with local payment methods (iDEAL Netherlands, Alipay China, Klarna Sweden); used by Uber, Spotify, Microsoft
BNPL: Affirm (longer loans with interest), Klarna (Pay in 4 zero-interest), Afterpay; merchant pays 4-6% but sees 20-85% basket size increase
Fraud costs: U.S. card-not-present fraud exceeds $9B annually; chargebacks cost transaction + $15-100 fee + merchandise
False declines cost more than fraud: Javelin Research estimates $443B in global false declines; every merchant must tune precision/recall of fraud rules
lecture_notes.demonstration: Open the Stripe dashboard in test mode and walk through creating a Payment Intent with an API call, viewing the transaction flow including auth vs capture, examining a Radar fraud score, issuing a refund, and viewing the Balance and Payouts section showing T+2 settlement timing. Then pull up public benchmarks: Shopify reports Shop Pay converts 1.72x better than guest checkout. Klarna reports 45% basket size increase. Calculate expected lift for a $10M store with 2% conversion: adding Shop Pay at 1.72x could increase revenue by ~$12M/year if lift holds on all traffic.
setup_requirements: Free Stripe test account (stripe.com)
Postman or cURL for API testing
Shopify test store with Shop Pay and Klarna enabled
Spreadsheet for payment economics calculation

review.value: Most of the 2.9% Stripe charges goes to Visa/Mastercard and the issuing bank as interchange; Stripe keeps roughly 0.3-0.5%. This is why Stripe's gross margins are lower than SaaS: they are a pass-through business on top of card networks

review.key: Conversion Rate Matters More Than Fees
review.value: Choosing PayPal because it costs 0.59% more than Stripe is the wrong analysis. If PayPal increases checkout conversion by 5% because customers trust it, the revenue gain swamps the fee difference. Always optimize for total revenue, not processor cost

When a customer pays with Affirm, the merchant gets paid in full immediately; Affirm takes the credit risk. This is why BNPL fees (4-6%) are higher than card fees (2.9%) - the BNPL provider is underwriting a consumer loan

A rule blocking all transactions from Nigeria stops fraud but loses legitimate customers; a rule that allows everything captures all revenue but absorbs fraud losses. The optimal threshold is a continuously tuned curve, not a static decision

class_schedule.topic: Fulfillment, Logistics, and the Last Mile
learning_objectives: Map the fulfillment chain from order placement to doorstep delivery, identifying each handoff and cost
Compare in-house fulfillment, 3PL providers, and Amazon FBA on cost, speed, and control trade-offs
Calculate landed cost and per-order profitability including pick-pack, shipping, and returns
Analyze the last-mile delivery problem and emerging solutions (Instacart, Shipt, gig networks, locker pickup)
readings: Chopra, Supply Chain Management: Strategy, Planning, and Operation (7th ed.), Ch. 14 - Transportation in a Supply Chain
Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 9 - Online Retail and Services
MIT Center for Transportation & Logistics, 'State of Supply Chain Sustainability 2023'
Wayfair Engineering, 'How Wayfair Delivers 20 Million SKUs' (public tech talk, 2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: Jeff Bezos once said that Amazon is not a retail company but a logistics company that happens to sell things, and the numbers back him up: Amazon spends over $90 billion annually on shipping and fulfillment, operates more than 1,500 facilities in the U.S., and has built private air and ground networks that rival FedEx and UPS. At the other end, a D2C mattress company ships one heavy item at a time and learns brutally that per-unit shipping can consume 15-25% of revenue. The fulfillment and logistics layer is where e-commerce business models either become profitable or collapse. Wayfair sells 20 million SKUs and has built 17 fulfillment centers plus a proprietary last-mile network called CastleGate specifically because UPS and FedEx cannot handle its bulky items economically. Instacart built a $39 billion valuation at peak around grocery last-mile, using gig-worker shoppers at Kroger and Costco; its 2023 IPO revealed that unit economics still depended on customer fees, retailer margins, and ads to be profitable. This session covers the real economics of getting physical goods from warehouse to doorstep: pick-pack costs, packaging, parcel rates, zones, dimensional weight, returns processing, and the brutal math of free shipping. By the end you will calculate the true per-order profit of a hypothetical D2C brand and see why so many scale-before-economics brands die at this layer.
key_concepts: Fulfillment chain: inbound receiving -> storage -> pick -> pack -> label -> ship -> last mile -> customer; each step has cost and error risk
In-house fulfillment: merchant runs own warehouse; highest control, lowest variable cost at scale, but high fixed costs and capex
3PL (third-party logistics): ShipBob, ShipHero, Flexport; $0.30-0.75 per item picked plus storage and shipping; ideal for $1M-$100M brands
Amazon FBA: inventory in Amazon's network, eligible for Prime; fulfillment fees $3-$6 per small/medium item + storage; conversion lift ~20-30%
Dropshipping: supplier ships directly to customer; zero inventory risk but zero margin (10-20%) and no quality control
Parcel carriers: USPS cheapest for small packages under 1 lb, UPS reliable ground, FedEx strong express, regional carriers OnTrac and LSO
Dimensional weight (DIM): large light packages billed by volume not actual weight; L*W*H/139 for UPS/FedEx; breaks economics of oversized items
Shipping zones: U.S. parcel carriers bill by zones 1-8; coast-to-coast is zone 8 and 3-4x the cost of zone 2; multi-warehouse strategy avoids zone 8
Last mile: ~40-50% of total delivery cost; solutions include USPS handoff (SurePost), Amazon Flex gig drivers, locker networks, in-home delivery
Returns economics: 20-40% return rate on apparel, 5-10% on hard goods; processing cost $10-30 per return plus restocking plus markdown
lecture_notes.demonstration: Model the per-order profit of a hypothetical D2C candle brand: $45 average order, 40% gross margin before logistics. COGS $27. Pick/pack at 3PL $1.50. Packaging $2. Shipping to zone 5 at 1.2 lb $8.75 (customer sees free shipping). Payment processing $1.61. Return allowance 5% $2.25. Customer acquisition blended $18. Net per order: $45 - $27 - $1.50 - $2 - $8.75 - $1.61 - $2.25 - $18 = -$16.11 loss. Discuss what must change: raise AOV, drop CAC, increase margin, or eliminate free shipping. This is the death spiral most D2C brands experienced in 2022.
setup_requirements: Per-order profit calculation spreadsheet template
Access to ShipStation demo or UPS/USPS rate calculators
Case study packet: Casper, Warby Parker, Wayfair, Amazon FBA fee schedule
Optional virtual tour of ShipBob or local 3PL warehouse

review.value: Free shipping at checkout means the merchant absorbs the cost. Stores that advertise free shipping typically raise product prices ~10% to compensate, or set minimum order thresholds, or use shipping as a marketing loss-leader balanced by repeat purchase

review.key: Amazon FBA Is a Conversion Bet
review.value: FBA fees are higher than most 3PLs, but products display a Prime badge that converts 20-30% better. The decision to use FBA vs 3PL is really about where your customers come from - if they are Amazon shoppers, FBA wins; if they are Shopify direct, a 3PL is cheaper

Zappos built its reputation on free returns; Amazon made returns effortless. Brands that make returns painful lose repeat purchase even when product quality is high. The return experience is part of the product

The first 1,000 miles of parcel delivery cost pennies per ton-mile; the last mile costs dollars per package. This is why Amazon built its own delivery network - at scale, the last mile is both the biggest cost and the biggest opportunity for differentiation

learning_objectives: Calculate customer acquisition cost (CAC) and lifetime value (LTV) and the LTV:CAC ratio for a real e-commerce business
Compare acquisition channels: SEO, paid search, paid social, email, affiliate, and organic content
Explain the iOS 14.5 ATT impact on paid social attribution and the shift toward first-party data
Build a blended CAC model across channels and analyze marginal return by channel
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 6 - E-commerce Marketing and Advertising Concepts
Chaffey, Digital Business and E-commerce Management (7th ed.), Ch. 8 - Digital Marketing
Andrew Chen, 'The Law of Shitty Clickthroughs' (2013) and 'How to Solve the Cold Start Problem' (2021)
Shopify Plus Guide to CAC and LTV (2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2012, acquiring a customer on Facebook cost a D2C brand around $8-$12. By 2022, the same customer cost $40-$80. This seven-fold increase, driven by rising ad-auction competition, Apple's App Tracking Transparency in iOS 14.5, and the retreat of third-party cookies, has reshaped the economics of every e-commerce business. Brands that scaled by buying customers at a loss, betting on lifetime value, found the bet no longer worked; Allbirds, Warby Parker, Casper, and Wayfair all reported material margin pressure as CAC rose. Customer acquisition is the most important topic in e-commerce economics because it is typically the largest variable cost after COGS. The LTV:CAC ratio - lifetime value divided by cost to acquire - is the single metric that determines whether your business is a sustainable enterprise or a cash-burning hype machine. Investors want LTV:CAC of 3:1 or higher with a payback period under 12 months. This session teaches you to calculate these numbers honestly, evaluate channels by marginal return not blended average, and think about the shift from paid acquisition to organic moats. Dollar Shave Club famously launched with one $4,500 video that got 12,000 subscribers in 48 hours - a CAC of $0.38. That is the kind of asymmetric outcome that makes organic content worth pursuing even when paid ads are easier to scale.
key_concepts: CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost): total marketing + sales spend divided by new customers acquired; blended or by channel
LTV (Lifetime Value): total gross profit from a customer; calculated as AOV x gross margin x purchase frequency x lifespan or using cohort retention curves
LTV:CAC ratio: 3:1 common benchmark for healthy SaaS and e-commerce; below 1:1 loses money; above 5:1 may indicate under-investment in growth
Payback period: months of gross profit before CAC recovered; e-commerce targets under 12 months; Allbirds reported ~18 months in 2022 as warning sign
SEO: Wirecutter, Healthline, Investopedia - long-term high-ROI but slow; algorithm dependency risk (Google core updates)
Paid search: Google Ads on brand terms (nearly free) vs generic terms ($5-$50+ CPC); high intent but competitive
Paid social: Meta, TikTok, Pinterest, Snap; historically cheap and scalable but disrupted by iOS 14.5
Email marketing: Klaviyo, Mailchimp - owned channel with 40:1 ROI per DMA but cannot scale past list size
Affiliate marketing: Wirecutter, Honey, Rakuten - pay per action, 5-15% of sale; works for high-intent products
Organic content and community: Glossier, Gymshark, Duolingo TikTok - leveraged, built on brand and social proof, most defensible
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a full LTV:CAC model for a hypothetical skincare brand in Google Sheets. Inputs: $60 AOV, 55% gross margin, 30% 12-month repeat rate, 3-year lifespan. LTV = $60 * 0.55 * (1 + 0.30 + 0.09 + 0.027) * 3 years ~ $46.86. Layer on CAC by channel: Google brand $5 (9.4:1), Google non-brand $42, Meta prospecting $55, TikTok $38, email $2, affiliate $18. Discuss: why would you ever spend on a 1.23:1 channel? Because brand search is saturated and cannot scale - volume is the constraint at profitable channels and you pay for growth via weaker channels at the margin. This is the core insight of marginal channel economics.
setup_requirements: Google Sheets for LTV/CAC model
Meta Ads Library (www.facebook.com/ads/library) for competitive creative research
Shopify free LTV calculator or spreadsheet template
SimilarWeb or SEMrush free tier for traffic source analysis

review.value: A single blended CAC hides 10x variation between channels. Branded search may be 20:1 while cold social prospecting is 0.8:1. Marketing leaders optimize the channel mix, not a single blended number

Apple's ATT eliminated per-user tracking on iOS, blinding Meta's ad targeting and attribution. CAC on Meta roughly doubled for many D2C brands in 2021-2022. Any business dependent on third-party platforms for CAC has platform risk

Paid channels rent customers; organic channels (SEO, content, community, brand) build an asset. Dollar Shave Club's launch video, Glossier's Instagram community, Duolingo's TikTok persona replaced millions in ad spend with a fraction of the cost

review.key: Payback Period Is the Cash Constraint
review.value: A 24-month payback period with 3:1 LTV:CAC is theoretically profitable but may kill the company via cash flow. If you spend $60 on CAC and earn it back over 24 months, you need $60 of working capital per new customer. Growth and cash are different problems

class_schedule.topic: Conversion Rate Optimization and Checkout Design
learning_objectives: Map the conversion funnel from landing to purchase, identifying typical drop-off points and benchmark rates
Apply heuristic and usability principles to diagnose conversion issues on real e-commerce sites
Design and analyze an A/B test for a specific checkout element
Explain why checkout abandonment averages 70%+ and which interventions have measurable lift
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 6 - E-commerce Marketing (conversion section)
Baymard Institute, 'E-commerce Checkout Usability Report' (2023)
Nielsen Norman Group, 'E-commerce User Experience' articles (nngroup.com)
Google Analytics 4 documentation on funnel analysis and e-commerce events

lecture_notes.introduction: The average e-commerce site has a conversion rate between 1% and 3%. Shopify's benchmark data across 1 million+ stores shows the median is 1.4%. This means 97-99 out of every 100 visitors leave without buying. Baymard Institute, after studying checkout flows for 11 years, found that 70% of carts are abandoned at checkout - and 17% of U.S. shoppers have abandoned an order in the last quarter specifically because the checkout was too long or complicated. A 1% improvement in conversion rate on a $10M store is $100K; on Amazon's $500B+ retail volume, it is $5B. Conversion rate optimization (CRO) is the highest-leverage work in e-commerce marketing because it compounds with every other investment: if you spent $1M on ads to drive 1M visitors and converted at 1.5%, you got 15,000 orders; improving to 1.8% gets you 18,000 orders from the same $1M of traffic - a free 20% lift. This session covers the psychology and mechanics of online conversion: the landing page, the product page, the cart, the checkout, and the post-purchase confirmation. You will learn Baymard's 120+ checkout usability guidelines, understand why guest checkout is worth $260B in recovered revenue globally, and build an A/B test that could actually hold up to peer review. CRO is not opinion; it is experimentation with real money on the line.
key_concepts: Conversion funnel: landing -> product page -> add to cart -> checkout start -> shipping info -> payment -> confirmation; typical drop-off 30-60% per stage
Benchmark conversion rates: Shopify median 1.4%, top-quartile 3%+, top-decile 5%+; vary by category and traffic source
Landing page optimization: clear value prop, hero product, social proof, one primary CTA above the fold
Product page conversion drivers: high-quality images, video, reviews (Bazaarvoice, Yotpo), stock urgency, size confidence, clear shipping and return policy
Cart abandonment drivers (Baymard): unexpected shipping costs 48%, account creation required 24%, long checkout 17%, not enough payment methods 9%, card declined 4%
Guest checkout: Baymard found forcing account creation loses 24% of potential buyers; optional post-purchase account creation is best practice
Single-page vs multi-step checkout: Baymard recommends multi-step because it reduces perceived complexity and isolates errors
Address autofill: Google Places API or Loqate reduces typing effort; can lift conversion 1-3%
A/B testing statistical rigor: sample size 10K-100K visitors per variant, p<0.05 significance, multiple testing corrections
Personalization and sequential testing: Optimizely, VWO, Google Optimize (sunset 2023); test one change per test, set hypotheses before running
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a full Baymard-style usability review of a real e-commerce site. Identify 10 specific friction points: missing product video, unclear shipping info on PDP, required account creation, small CTAs. Then build an A/B test hypothesis: 'Adding guest checkout will increase checkout completion by at least 10%.' Calculate required sample size: for baseline 40% conversion and target 44%, with 80% power and alpha 0.05, we need roughly 5,400 visitors per variant. Show how to implement, define success metrics, and duration required.
setup_requirements: Target e-commerce site for usability review
Baymard 12-step usability checklist handout
A/B test sample size calculator (Evan Miller or Optimizely)
Spreadsheet for test result analysis

review.value: A 20% conversion rate improvement is equivalent to a 20% traffic increase, but cheaper and permanent. Every dollar spent driving traffic benefits from every conversion improvement made in parallel
48% of cart abandonments are caused by unexpected shipping costs at checkout. Brands that show shipping early see materially higher conversion than brands that surprise at checkout
Most A/B tests fail to reach significance because they are stopped early or run on insufficient traffic. A test declared a winner at p=0.04 after 3 days often regresses at p=0.30 when run properly

A small D2C store with 50 conversions/day cannot reliably detect a 5% lift without running a test for 3+ months. This is why CRO at small scale focuses on qualitative research (session recordings, user testing) instead of quantitative experiments

learning_objectives: Distinguish between collaborative filtering, content-based, and hybrid recommendation approaches
Explain how Amazon's item-to-item collaborative filtering produces 'customers who bought this also bought' recommendations
Evaluate search relevance using precision, recall, and click-through-rate on no-result queries
Design a personalization strategy that respects user privacy while delivering measurable lift
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 6 - Personalization section
Linden, Smith, and York, 'Amazon.com Recommendations: Item-to-Item Collaborative Filtering' (IEEE Internet Computing, 2003)
Netflix Tech Blog, 'The Netflix Recommender System' (public papers, 2016 onward)
Algolia Documentation: Search Relevance and Personalization

lecture_notes.introduction: When you visit Amazon, almost every pixel is personalized: the homepage banners reflect your browsing history, the carousels show products similar to ones you viewed, the search results rank differently from the anonymous view, and the checkout page upsells you on items the algorithm predicts you will add. Amazon's former CEO Jeff Wilke estimated that 35% of Amazon's revenue comes from the recommendation engine. Netflix's executives have credited their recommendation system with retaining over $1 billion per year in subscriptions by keeping viewers engaged. Personalization is not a cosmetic feature; it is a revenue-generating system that, done well, can lift conversion by 10-30% and average order value by 15-20%. But personalization is also where privacy tensions are hottest: Target's pregnancy prediction case (where an angry father discovered his teenage daughter was pregnant because Target started sending baby ads before she had told her family) remains the cautionary story of what can go wrong when algorithms optimize without a conscience. This session covers both the mathematics of recommendation systems and the ethical and practical boundaries of personalization. You will understand how collaborative filtering works, why Amazon moved from user-to-user to item-to-item filtering, how Algolia and Elasticsearch power e-commerce search, and how modern systems balance relevance, diversity, and fairness.
key_concepts: Collaborative filtering user-to-user: find users similar to target, recommend items they bought; computationally expensive at scale
Collaborative filtering item-to-item: precompute similar items based on co-purchase; Amazon's breakthrough because item similarities are more stable
Content-based filtering: recommend items whose attributes match user history; handles cold start but can be narrow
Hybrid approaches: combine collaborative and content-based with ML ranking; Netflix uses hundreds of signals
Cold start problem: new users and new items cannot use collaborative filtering; solutions include content-based fallbacks, onboarding, popularity baselines
Search relevance metrics: CTR on results, zero-result query rate (<2% healthy, >5% problems), conversion from search
Typo tolerance and query expansion: Levenshtein distance 2-3 edits, synonym expansion (sofa -> couch)
Faceted search: filters by category, price, size, color, brand with dynamic counts
Ranking signals: BM25 text match, popularity, recency, business rules, personalization, merchandising
Privacy and personalization trade-off: more data = better recs = higher conversion, but more surveillance risk and regulatory exposure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through Amazon's live personalization: view a product, return to homepage to see it in 'Recently viewed', check 'Customers who bought this also bought' on the PDP, see 'Inspired by your browsing history' in checkout. Contrast with an anonymous incognito browser. Then open Algolia's demo store and show search features: instant search as you type, typo tolerance (type 'blak dres' and find 'black dress'), faceted filters, analytics dashboard showing top queries and zero-result queries.
setup_requirements: Amazon account for live personalization demo
Incognito browser for anonymous comparison
Algolia demo store or free trial account
Sample product catalog and query log for analysis

review.value: Amazon's key insight was that item similarities change slowly and can be precomputed, while user similarities change every purchase. This enabled real-time recommendations at Amazon's scale in the early 2000s when it would have been impossible otherwise

Users who use site search convert 2-3x better than users who only browse. Every dollar spent improving search returns far more than a dollar spent on homepage redesigns. Yet most e-commerce sites treat search as an afterthought
A zero-result query is a customer telling you exactly what they want and discovering you do not have it. Amazon analyzes these to find product expansion opportunities; weak sites miss them entirely

The first 3-5 personalization signals capture most of the lift. Adding 100 more marginal signals rarely moves the needle more than a few basis points while multiplying privacy and engineering complexity

learning_objectives: Explain PCI-DSS requirements and how payment tokenization keeps merchants out of scope
Map the GDPR (EU), CCPA/CPRA (California), and emerging state privacy laws to e-commerce obligations
Identify common security threats: card testing, account takeover, scraping, and DDoS, and their mitigations
Evaluate trust signals on e-commerce sites (SSL, reviews, badges, return policy) and their conversion impact
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 5 - E-commerce Security and Payment Systems
PCI Security Standards Council, 'PCI DSS Quick Reference Guide' (current version)
EU GDPR Official Text (Articles 6, 7, 13, 17, 32) and ICO guidance
Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report (annual, 2023 edition)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2013, Target was breached through an HVAC contractor's credentials, and the attackers stole 40 million credit card numbers from Target's POS systems. The incident cost Target over $200 million in settlements, the CEO's job, and years of reputational damage. In 2017, Equifax lost 147 million credit records through an unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability; the settlement exceeded $1.4 billion. Every year, the Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report shows that e-commerce and payment processing are among the most attacked industries because that is where the money and personal data live. Trust is both the hardest and most valuable asset in e-commerce: Amazon's market dominance is fundamentally about trust (returns work, orders arrive, payment data is safe), while a single high-profile breach can destroy a smaller brand. This session covers the three pillars of e-commerce trust: technical security, regulatory compliance, and user-perceived trust. You will learn why Stripe and Shopify exist partly to carry the compliance burden on behalf of merchants, why the EU's right-to-be-forgotten has practical engineering implications, and why trust signals like SSL lock icons and customer reviews can lift conversion by double digits.
key_concepts: PCI-DSS: 12 requirements for any merchant storing, processing, or transmitting card data; SAQ A through D depending on scope
Tokenization: replacing card numbers with opaque tokens so merchant never touches raw PAN; reduces PCI scope to SAQ A
SSL/TLS: encrypts data in transit; universal due to Let's Encrypt free certificates and Chrome warnings for HTTP sites
GDPR: requires lawful basis, explicit consent, right of access, right to erasure, 72-hour breach notification; fines up to 4% global revenue
CCPA/CPRA: right to know, right to delete, right to opt out, non-discrimination; extends GDPR-style protections to California
State law patchwork: Virginia, Colorado, Connecticut, Utah, Texas, Oregon passing comprehensive privacy laws
Card testing attack: attackers use stolen cards in small test transactions to validate them; signs include many low-value declines from same IP
Account takeover (ATO): credential stuffing from leaked passwords; mitigations include MFA, rate limiting, bot detection
Scraping: competitors and bots scrape prices, images, data; mitigations include rate limiting, CAPTCHAs, WAF rules
DDoS protection: Cloudflare, AWS Shield, Akamai; the 2016 Dyn attack via Mirai took down Twitter, Netflix, and many e-commerce sites
lecture_notes.demonstration: Open Shopify's checkout and point out visible trust signals: SSL padlock, payment method logos, trust badges, return policy link, contact info, review snippets. Then demonstrate a GDPR cookie consent banner on a European site showing granular consent categories. Finally, walk through a sample PCI SAQ A questionnaire to show what a merchant must attest to when using Stripe: roughly 20 yes/no questions instead of 300+ in SAQ D.
setup_requirements: Sample PCI SAQ A document for review
GDPR-compliant cookie banner example (OneTrust, Cookiebot)
Have I Been Pwned lookup for credential stuffing discussion
Cloudflare demo dashboard for WAF and bot detection

review.value: By never touching the real card number, a merchant collapses PCI scope from SAQ D (hundreds of controls) to SAQ A (a short attestation). This is why Stripe and Shopify Payments exist and why building your own card handling is almost always wrong

Amazon was fined 746 million Euro by Luxembourg for GDPR violations in 2021. Meta, Google, and WhatsApp have been fined hundreds of millions. The 'no one enforces this' argument is wrong and getting more wrong each year
A Baymard study found that a visible SSL badge, customer reviews, clear return policy, and contact information together lift conversion 15-25%. Trust is operationally cheap and commercially priceless

review.key: Your Weakest Link Is Your Vendors
review.value: Target was breached through its HVAC contractor. Equifax was breached through an unpatched third-party library. Every vendor with access to your systems is a potential entry point, which is why SOC 2 compliance and vendor security reviews matter

class_schedule.topic: Mobile Commerce and Progressive Web Apps
learning_objectives: Analyze the shift from desktop to mobile commerce and the implications for design, speed, and checkout
Compare mobile app, mobile web, and progressive web app (PWA) strategies with real company examples
Apply Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) to diagnose mobile performance problems
Design a mobile checkout using Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Shop Pay to maximize conversion
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 3 - The E-commerce Marketplace and Ch. 4 - Mobile sections
Google, 'Why Performance Matters' and Core Web Vitals documentation (web.dev)
Chaffey, Digital Business and E-commerce Management (7th ed.), Ch. 7 - Delivering the digital customer experience
AliExpress PWA case study and Twitter Lite case study (Google developers blog)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2016, mobile traffic crossed 50% of all e-commerce traffic for the first time in the U.S. By 2023, mobile accounted for 67% of traffic and roughly 55% of revenue. That 12-point gap between traffic share and revenue share is one of the most important numbers in e-commerce: mobile visitors convert at roughly half the rate of desktop visitors, meaning if you fix mobile conversion you unlock billions in revenue. Why does mobile convert worse? Slow page loads, tiny forms, awkward address entry, hesitant payment methods, and checkout flows designed for mouse-and-keyboard instead of thumb-on-glass. Amazon's mobile app handled roughly half of its U.S. retail revenue in 2023, proving mobile can work when done right. This session covers the specific techniques for making mobile e-commerce not just tolerable but excellent: Core Web Vitals as Google's ranking and UX signal, Progressive Web Apps that deliver app-like experiences without App Store friction (AliExpress doubled conversion with PWA), and the mobile wallet revolution (Apple Pay, Google Pay, Shop Pay) that collapses 20-field checkouts into a single biometric tap. By the end, you will know why Shop Pay converts 1.72x better than guest checkout and what it takes to hit Google's performance standards.
key_concepts: Mobile vs desktop conversion gap: mobile ~2.5% vs desktop ~5% globally; biggest revenue lever in m-commerce
Native app vs mobile web: Amazon, Walmart, Target run both; native apps convert higher for repeat users but require install friction
Progressive Web App: web app with offline support, push notifications, home screen install; Lancome PWA 8% conversion lift, Twitter Lite 65% pages per session increase
Core Web Vitals: LCP under 2.5s, INP under 200ms (replaced FID 2024), CLS under 0.1
Performance budget: limits on page weight, JS bundle, image size; enforced in CI with Lighthouse or WebPageTest
Image optimization: srcset responsive images, WebP and AVIF formats, lazy loading, CDN resizing (Cloudinary, imgix)
Mobile wallets: Apple Pay, Google Pay, Shop Pay, Amazon Pay - all collapse checkout to 1-tap with biometric auth
Mobile UX principles: 44pt+ tap targets, single-column forms, autofill-friendly input types, sticky CTA
Push notifications: re-engagement channel; watch opt-in rates and frequency to avoid uninstalls
App store economics: Apple and Google take 15-30% of in-app purchases; physical goods exempt
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run Chrome Lighthouse on a real e-commerce site's mobile view. Review Core Web Vitals: identify LCP offenders (hero image or web fonts), CLS offenders (late-loading banners), INP issues (heavy JavaScript on tap). Then demonstrate Shop Pay checkout: tap 'Buy it now', authenticate biometric, order placed in 10 seconds. Contrast with traditional 5-step checkout. Pull up AliExpress PWA case study: bounce rate down 27%, time on site up 74%, iOS conversion up 82%.
setup_requirements: Chrome DevTools + Lighthouse
Real device (phone) for Apple Pay / Google Pay testing
Shopify test store with Shop Pay enabled
web.dev Core Web Vitals tool bookmarked

review.key: Mobile Conversion Gap Is the Biggest Lever
review.value: Closing half of the mobile-desktop conversion gap at a business doing 60% mobile traffic is roughly a 15% revenue lift. No ad channel or new product launch matches this for a mature business
Google uses Core Web Vitals as a ranking signal. A slow site not only converts worse but ranks worse, compounding the damage

A merchant on Shopify who does not enable Shop Pay is leaving 1.5-2x mobile checkout conversion on the table. The network effect of saved credentials across Shopify merchants is the strongest reason to be on the platform
A native app costs $200K-$1M to build and only pays off with millions of repeat visitors. Small brands should do PWA or mobile web well before considering native

learning_objectives: Distinguish between replenishment, curation, and access subscription models with real company examples
Calculate subscription MRR, churn, and LTV and explain why these metrics drive valuation
Evaluate the economics of the Dollar Shave Club acquisition by Unilever and the Blue Apron IPO failure
Design a subscription offering with trial, pricing tiers, and anti-churn mechanisms
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 1 section on business models and Ch. 6 on customer retention
Zuora 'Subscription Economy Index' (2023 edition)
Harvard Business Review, 'How to Thrive in the Subscription Economy' (Tzuo, 2015)
McKinsey, 'Thinking Inside the Subscription Box' (2018 report on e-commerce subscriptions)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2012, Michael Dubin launched Dollar Shave Club with a $4,500 viral video and a simple pitch: instead of paying $25 for brand-name razors at CVS, pay $9 per month for decent razors delivered to your door. Five years later, Unilever acquired Dollar Shave Club for $1 billion. Same year, Blue Apron IPO'd at $10 per share with a $1.9 billion valuation; it now trades under $1 and was taken private. Both were subscription commerce businesses. Both seemed revolutionary. Only one worked. Why? Because subscription commerce is not a growth hack; it is a business model with specific economics, and the brands that understand those economics (durable replenishment categories, low churn, high LTV:CAC) win while those that don't (variable basket categories, high churn, punishing CAC) fail. Netflix, Spotify, and Amazon Prime turned subscription into the default monetization model for digital goods. Dollar Shave Club, HelloFresh, and Chewy autoship brought it to physical goods. This session covers the three flavors of subscription, the metrics that matter, and the hardest problem in subscription commerce: managing churn. A 5% monthly churn rate compounds to 46% annual churn - you have to replace half your customer base every year just to stand still.
key_concepts: Replenishment subscription: Dollar Shave Club, Chewy autoship, Amazon Subscribe & Save - automatic recurring delivery with discount
Curation subscription: Birchbox, Stitch Fix, HelloFresh, Bespoke Post - new experience each cycle, surprise-driven, higher churn
Access subscription: Netflix, Spotify, Amazon Prime, ClassPass, Peloton - unlimited access for flat fee; often bundles
MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue): sum of committed monthly revenue; grows via new, expansion, churn, downgrades
Churn: gross churn measures cancellations, net churn includes expansion; Netflix ~3%, Blue Apron peaked at 72% annual
CAC payback for subscriptions: 12-18 months benchmark, under 12 for healthy unit economics
LTV for subscription: (ARPU * gross margin) / churn rate; at $30 ARPU, 60% margin, 5% monthly churn, LTV = $360
Free trial vs paid trial: Netflix historically free, ended 2020; many now use $1 trials or reverse trials
Pause as anti-churn: allowing pause instead of cancel retains LTV; Dollar Shave Club, HelloFresh, Peloton all use this
Subscription fatigue: by 2023, average U.S. consumer had 4+ paid subscriptions and was actively pruning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Model a subscription business: monthly price $35, COGS $12, CAC $60, gross churn 4% monthly. LTV = ($35-$12) / 0.04 = $575. LTV:CAC = 9.6:1 (healthy). CAC payback = 2.6 months. Now at 10% churn: LTV $230, LTV:CAC 3.8:1. At 15%: LTV $153, LTV:CAC 2.56:1. At 25% (Blue Apron): LTV $92, LTV:CAC 1.53:1 (death). Discuss how churn is the dominant variable.
setup_requirements: Subscription model spreadsheet template
ChartMogul or Stripe Billing demo account for SaaS metric examples
Sample subscription brand checkout flows for comparison
Case study packet: Dollar Shave Club, Blue Apron, Peloton, Netflix

review.value: You can double CAC efficiency through better marketing; you cannot recover from 25% monthly churn through any amount of growth. Churn mathematically caps your business size, and retention work is strictly more valuable than acquisition in subscription
Curation has novelty appeal that fades, producing higher churn. Replenishment replaces a purchase the customer would make anyway. Chewy's autoship is the textbook example
Amazon Prime is a genius bundle: shipping + video + music + photos + Kindle + gaming. Each service might churn, but the bundle is sticky because canceling means losing everything
The average U.S. household spends $273/month on subscriptions, and churn has risen as consumers prune. New launches in 2025 face a more skeptical market than 2015

class_schedule.topic: Social Commerce, Live Shopping, and Influencer Economics
learning_objectives: Compare social commerce platforms (Instagram Shopping, TikTok Shop, Facebook Marketplace, Pinterest) on mechanics and results
Explain the live-streaming commerce model that drives 15%+ of China e-commerce via Taobao Live and Douyin
Evaluate influencer marketing economics including flat fees, affiliate models, and ambassador programs
Analyze why Shein and Temu grew to billions in U.S. GMV using TikTok-native acquisition
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 11 - Social Commerce
Chaffey, Digital Business and E-commerce Management (7th ed.), Ch. 9 - Social media marketing
McKinsey, 'Retail reimagined: The new era for customer experience in China's live streaming e-commerce' (2022)
Matt Stoller, BIG newsletter, 'The Shein and Temu model' (2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2020, Viya, China's top livestream shopping host, sold $3 billion in merchandise in one night during Alibaba's Singles Day. One night. One person. Using nothing but a smartphone camera, a ring light, and a passionate audience. Livestream commerce was a $480 billion category in China by 2023, representing over 15% of all Chinese e-commerce, while the U.S. equivalent (Amazon Live, QVC Live, TikTok Shop) remained well under 5%. Why the gap? Culture, platform integration, and the fact that WeChat Pay and Alipay built one-click in-stream purchase before Meta and YouTube had similar infrastructure. Meanwhile, TikTok Shop launched in the U.S. in 2023 and processed over $16 billion in GMV in its first 12 months, proving the model can cross borders. Shein and Temu, both Chinese-owned, have used TikTok-native influencer content and ultra-low prices to become two of the most downloaded apps in the U.S., with Temu spending an estimated $2 billion on Meta and Google ads in 2023 alone. This session covers the mechanics of social commerce, the economics of influencer marketing, and the structural forces behind Shein and Temu that have legacy retailers scrambling.
key_concepts: Instagram Shopping: product tags in posts/reels, links to brand site; default for D2C product discovery
TikTok Shop: U.S. launch 2023, in-app checkout, live shopping, affiliate program; rapidly scaling but regulatory risk
Pinterest: high purchase intent, strong for home, wedding, fashion; recently expanded Shopping and direct-checkout
Live shopping formats: scheduled live events with real-time commentary, limited-time offers, flash sales, Q&A
Douyin and Taobao Live: mature Chinese ecosystem with superstars (Li Jiaqi, Viya), category experts; double-digit percent of Chinese e-commerce
Influencer tiers: nano 1K-10K, micro 10K-100K, mid 100K-1M, macro 1M+, celebrity 10M+
Pricing: nano $10-100, micro $100-500, mid $500-5K, macro $5K-50K, celebrity $100K+; affiliate 5-20% commission
Content-led acquisition: Gymshark, Glossier, Fashion Nova built billion-dollar businesses on influencer and community content
Shein model: ultra-low prices $5-15, massive SKU velocity 10K new styles/day, data-driven design, de minimis loophole, TikTok distribution
Temu model: Pinduoduo parent, aggressive ad spend, factory-direct, gamified shopping (spinning wheels, discounts, referrals)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Open TikTok Shop in-app and walk through a live-selling session: host demonstrates products, viewers tap to buy without leaving the video, purchase completes in seconds with saved payment info. Contrast with traditional Instagram Shopping flow that links out to a merchant site. Then review Meta Ads Library for Temu and Shein: observe the volume (tens of thousands of creatives), messaging (urgency, discount, gamification), and targeting signals. Calculate influencer ROI: $500 micro-influencer post to 50K followers at 3% engagement = 1,500 interactions; at 2% click-to-site and 3% purchase, 0.9 sales - not enough alone, but 10 in parallel scales.
setup_requirements: TikTok app for live shopping demo
Meta Ads Library (www.facebook.com/ads/library) for competitive analysis
Instagram Shopping browsing experience
Sample influencer contract and rate card handout

review.value: Social commerce is a new form of retail where entertainment and transaction collapse into a single experience. Brands that treat TikTok as an ad channel miss the creator-driven, content-first nature of the medium

The U.S. live-shopping market is years behind China. If it converges to 10% penetration, that is $200B+ of GMV shift. Early movers (TikTok Shop, Amazon Live, YouTube Shopping) are betting on this convergence

review.key: Shein's Advantage Is Data and Speed
review.value: Shein's real moat is its supply chain: small initial runs, real-time demand sensing, factory-direct production in 7 days instead of 7 months. Low prices are enabled by the speed. This is Toyota production system applied to fast fashion

A single macro $50K post might produce 100 sales; ten micro $500 posts often produce 300-500 sales for the same $5K. Portfolio diversification and authenticity matter more than raw reach for most brands

learning_objectives: Evaluate the build-vs-partner decision for entering a new country (own store vs Amazon/Alibaba/Mercado Libre)
Map the regulatory landscape for cross-border commerce: VAT, customs duties, data localization, currency controls
Design a localization strategy covering language, currency, payment methods, and shipping
Compare Alibaba, JD.com, Amazon, and Mercado Libre as regional platform dominants
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 12 - B2B E-commerce and Ch. 1 sections on global commerce
Chaffey, Digital Business and E-commerce Management (7th ed.), Ch. 6 - Digital business strategy
McKinsey, 'The State of Fashion 2024' global chapter
PayPal, 'Global Insights: Cross-Border Shopping' (annual report)

lecture_notes.introduction: Amazon operates in 20+ countries but dominates only a few of them. In China, Amazon closed its domestic marketplace in 2019 after years of losing to Alibaba's Tmall and JD.com. In Brazil and Mexico, Amazon competes with Mercado Libre, which has local logistics advantages Amazon cannot match. In India, Flipkart (owned by Walmart) and Amazon split the market, but regulatory restrictions on foreign-owned e-commerce limit what either can do. Cross-border e-commerce is not a translation project; it is a series of local market entry decisions with regulatory, logistical, cultural, and competitive dimensions. A U.S. brand that wants to sell in Europe faces VAT in 27 different countries, GDPR, EU customs rules, local payment preferences (iDEAL in Netherlands, Klarna in Sweden, SEPA direct debit in Germany), and return address logistics. The same brand going to Japan faces fundamentally different UX expectations, preferred payment methods (konbini convenience store payment, cash on delivery), and the dominance of Rakuten. This session covers the strategic frameworks for international expansion: when to use marketplaces vs build, how to handle tax and customs, how to localize without fragmenting your brand, and the hidden operational costs that surprise every first-time international seller.
key_concepts: Global e-commerce is not homogeneous: Amazon ~40% U.S., Alibaba dominant China, Mercado Libre LatAm, Flipkart/Amazon India, Rakuten Japan, Lazada/Shopee SEA
Build vs partner: own localized store gives brand control but requires investment; marketplace gives instant distribution but platform dependence
VAT in Europe: 27 member states with rates 17-27%; OSS scheme since 2021 simplifies reporting to one EU registration up to 10,000 Euro threshold
U.S. sales tax post-Wayfair (2018): economic nexus thresholds ($100K or 200 transactions) trigger tax collection in 45+ states; Avalara and TaxJar automate
De minimis thresholds: U.S. Section 321 allows duty-free under $800, exploited by Shein and Temu; EU eliminated 22 Euro threshold in 2021
Payment method localization: credit cards dominate U.S.; Europe uses iDEAL, SEPA, Klarna; China uses Alipay, WeChat Pay; Brazil uses PIX and boleto
Currency display: show local currency to reduce abandonment; FX markup 2-3% when settled in USD
Data localization: GDPR in EU, Russia on-shore, China PIPL; may require in-country storage and increase infrastructure cost
Shipping and customs: Incoterms (DDP, DAP, EXW) determine who pays duty and taxes; DDP best for customer experience
Cultural localization: date formats, address formats, product preferences, imagery, payment cycles
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through three international e-commerce experiences: Nike.com.br vs Nike.com (prices in BRL, PIX payment, different product mix, installment pricing), Amazon.co.jp (Japanese UX density, Rakuten-style rich PDP, konbini payment), and Shein entering Brazil (aggressive Portuguese localization, local warehouses announced 2023). Then show a Shopify Markets dashboard where a merchant can toggle international markets, set local pricing, and configure duties display.
setup_requirements: Shopify Markets demo or screenshots
Browser for international Amazon/Nike/Shein comparisons
Avalara or TaxJar sample tax calculation
Incoterms reference chart

review.value: Translating your English site into French does not make it work in France. It needs French payment methods, shipping expectations, return addresses, customer service, marketing, and tax registration. First-time expansion underestimates this 5-10x

In China, joining Tmall is almost mandatory for reaching Chinese consumers; in Japan, Rakuten offers the same role; in Brazil, Mercado Libre. Trying to build a standalone brand site in these markets usually fails

A merchant that sells 500K Euro into Europe without registering for VAT faces back-taxes, penalties, and potential criminal liability. VAT handling is the biggest compliance trap and must be solved before revenue, not after

review.key: Shein and Temu Exploited a Loophole
review.value: The $800 U.S. de minimis threshold allows Chinese sellers to ship directly to U.S. consumers duty-free, bypassing tariffs U.S. retailers pay. Congressional action to close this loophole is ongoing

learning_objectives: Define omnichannel vs multichannel and explain why unified customer data is the crux
Evaluate BOPIS (buy online pickup in store), curbside, and ship-from-store as inventory-leverage strategies
Compare Walmart's omnichannel transformation to Amazon's physical retail expansion
Design a POS + e-commerce integration covering inventory, customer, and loyalty data unification
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 9 - Online Retail (omnichannel sections)
Chaffey, Digital Business and E-commerce Management (7th ed.), Ch. 6 - Multichannel strategy
Doug McMillon, Walmart Annual Letters (2019-2023) on omnichannel transformation
Harvard Business Review, 'How to Win in an Omnichannel World' (Rigby, 2011 foundational article)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2016, Walmart acquired Jet.com for $3.3 billion, installed founder Marc Lore as head of U.S. e-commerce, and began a decade-long transformation from the world's largest brick-and-mortar retailer into a genuine omnichannel company. Jet.com has since been shut down, but the infrastructure it seeded built Walmart's e-commerce from $14B in 2016 to over $80B in 2023, with BOPIS and pickup grocery as the pointy end of the wedge. Walmart's strategic insight was that its 4,700 U.S. stores were not a liability in the Amazon age but the biggest asset: every store is within 10 miles of 90% of Americans, every store could be a fulfillment center, and every store could handle returns and pickups cheaper than any package network. Target followed the same playbook with its Shipt acquisition. Best Buy rebuilt as an electronics advisor-plus-pickup. Meanwhile, Amazon bought Whole Foods for $13.7 billion to get physical grocery presence. The line between online and offline retail has effectively dissolved for the biggest players. This session covers the mechanics of omnichannel: inventory visibility across channels, customer identity across touchpoints, BOPIS operations, returns across channels, and the data and IT systems that make it work.
key_concepts: Multichannel vs omnichannel: multichannel sells through multiple channels independently; omnichannel unifies customer, inventory, and order data
Unified customer record: single profile combining online purchases, in-store, email engagement, app usage, loyalty
Unified inventory: real-time visibility across warehouses and stores; allows online order shipped from closest store
BOPIS (Buy Online Pickup In Store): higher margin than ship-to-home (no shipping cost) and drives ~30% additional in-store purchases
Curbside pickup: customer never enters the store; exploded during COVID-19 and remained popular for grocery
Ship-from-store: online orders fulfilled from store inventory, leveraging stores as mini-DCs
Endless aisle: in-store kiosks let customers order out-of-stock items for home delivery
Returns across channels: customer bought online but returns in store; improves experience but requires reconciliation
POS integration: Shopify POS, Square, Lightspeed, NCR sync to e-commerce platform
Attribution challenges: customer browses in-store, researches on mobile, buys online - who gets credit? Unified loyalty IDs and last-touch-with-context
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through Target's app experience: browse, add to cart, choose Pickup at a specific store, see real-time inventory at that store, place order, receive notification when ready, arrive at store and tap 'I'm here' in the app, receive order within 1 minute. This experience depends on store inventory API, customer identity across app and POS, order management system, and store operations. Contrast with Shopify POS Pro which offers similar capabilities out of the box.
setup_requirements: Target or Walmart app for live demo
Shopify POS Pro demo or screenshots
Case study packet: Walmart, Target, Best Buy omnichannel transformations
Sample OMS (order management system) dashboard screenshots

review.value: The Amazon-era assumption that physical stores were a liability reversed completely. Walmart's 4,700 stores within 10 miles of most Americans became the biggest competitive advantage in the omnichannel era

review.key: Unified Data Is the Hard Part
review.value: The technology for BOPIS and ship-from-store exists and can be bought. The hard part is merging customer, inventory, and order data from 20 years of separate systems. Macy's and Kohl's struggled because of data fragmentation
BOPIS eliminates shipping cost $6-10 per order and generates additional in-store purchases ~30% of the time. On a $50 online order, this is the difference between break-even and 20% margin

Walmart's transformation took seven years of sustained investment. Mid-market retailers cannot buy this in a six-month project; it requires multi-year commitment and willingness to fail on smaller experiments

learning_objectives: Analyze the power dynamics between marketplace operators and third-party sellers using Amazon as the case study
Evaluate Amazon private label expansion and its implications for third-party sellers
Compare Amazon Seller Central and Vendor Central economics and when to use each
Develop a platform diversification strategy to reduce marketplace concentration risk
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 1 section on marketplaces and Ch. 9 on online retail
Stacy Mitchell, 'Amazon's Stranglehold' (ILSR, 2016)
U.S. House Judiciary Subcommittee, 'Investigation of Competition in Digital Markets' (2020)
Marketplace Pulse newsletters, State of Amazon Marketplace annual reports

lecture_notes.introduction: Amazon has over 2 million third-party sellers who collectively drive more than 60% of Amazon retail unit sales. For many of those sellers, Amazon is not a channel; it is the business. And that is precisely the problem. Amazon controls the rules, the rankings, the fees, the search results, and increasingly the inventory itself. A 2019 internal Amazon document, reported in the Wall Street Journal, showed Amazon used third-party seller data to inform its own private-label product launches. When Amazon Basics batteries undercut Energizer by 30% and got promoted to the top of search results, the independent battery seller had no recourse. The European Commission, the U.S. FTC, and multiple Congressional investigations have all examined whether Amazon's dual role as marketplace operator and competing seller is anticompetitive. In 2023, the FTC filed suit against Amazon. Meanwhile, sellers have learned the hard way that Amazon suspensions can kill a business overnight, that FBA fee increases can wipe out margin, and that algorithm changes can drop rankings by 70% without warning. This session covers the uncomfortable economics of selling on marketplaces: the power imbalance, the private-label threat, the suspension risk, and the diversification strategies that sophisticated sellers use to survive.
key_concepts: Amazon 3P seller economics: referral fee 8-15% + FBA fee $3-6 per unit + storage + advertising + returns; total take-rate 30-50%
Seller Central vs Vendor Central: Seller Central direct-to-consumer with you owning relationship; Vendor Central Amazon buys wholesale
Private label competition: Amazon Basics, Amazon Essentials, Happy Belly, Pinzon compete with 3P sellers with algorithmic preference
Buy Box: default Add to Cart position owned by one seller at a time based on price/shipping/metrics; losing it loses 90%+ of sales
Suspension risk: Amazon can suspend for policy violations, safety complaints, algorithmic flags; restoration takes weeks
Advertising inflation: Sponsored Products, Brands, Display; ad spend rose from ~5% in 2015 to 15-20% in 2023
FTC v. Amazon (2023): alleges anti-discounting measures, FBA coercion, and unfair marketplace data use
Alternative marketplaces: Walmart.com, Target Plus, eBay, Etsy, Faire, Mercado Libre, Allegro
Diversification playbook: own site on Shopify, 2-3 marketplaces, email list, social following, wholesale; no channel exceeds 40-50%
Brand escape: Anker, Otterbox, Peak Design started on Amazon and built brand equity for direct traffic
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a hypothetical seller on Amazon: $25 product, 30% gross margin before Amazon fees. Referral 15% $3.75. FBA $4.50. Advertising 15% $3.75. Return allowance 3% $0.75. Total Amazon costs $12.75 out of $25 = 51% take-rate. With $17.50 COGS, net = $25 - $12.75 - $17.50 = -$5.25 (loss). Discuss: why do sellers tolerate this? Because the alternative is $0 revenue if they cannot drive traffic to a standalone store. Show Marketplace Pulse chart of Amazon advertising as % of 3P revenue over time.
setup_requirements: Amazon Seller Central demo account or screenshots
Amazon FBA fee calculator
Marketplace Pulse newsletters and charts
Case study: Anker's escape from marketplace dependence

review.value: If 80% of your revenue comes from one marketplace, you do not own a business; you rent access to one. Amazon, Google, and Meta have all proven willing to change rules that destroy dependent businesses overnight
When your product becomes popular on Amazon, you have revealed exactly what to build under its own brand. Sellers in commodity categories are structurally vulnerable; brand-driven categories less so
As more sellers bid for Sponsored Products placement, cost per click rises, eating into margin. This is a commons tragedy: rational individual behavior collectively destroys category economics

The only way to escape marketplace dependence is to build alternatives: own site, other marketplaces, email, social, retail. Sellers who start diversifying when their Amazon business is strong succeed; those who wait until suspension are too late

class_schedule.topic: The Future of E-commerce: AI, AR, and New Business Models
learning_objectives: Evaluate current and near-term applications of generative AI in e-commerce
Analyze augmented reality applications in fashion (Warby Parker), furniture (IKEA Place), and beauty (Sephora)
Forecast the impact of zero-click commerce (voice, conversational AI, connected devices)
Synthesize the course themes into a strategic framework for building a defensible e-commerce business
readings: Laudon & Traver, E-commerce 2024, Ch. 14 - Online Content and Media
Andreessen Horowitz, 'The AI Wave in Commerce' (public essays, 2023-2024)
McKinsey, 'The Economic Potential of Generative AI' (2023)
Chaffey, Digital Business and E-commerce Management (7th ed.), Ch. 10 - Future of digital business

lecture_notes.introduction: The history of e-commerce is the history of reducing friction. Every decade, a new technology has stripped out another layer of cost or inconvenience: the web browser eliminated catalog ordering, the credit card gateway eliminated mailing checks, one-click eliminated form-filling, mobile wallets eliminated card entry, same-day delivery eliminated waiting, and now AI is stripping out the cognitive cost of shopping itself. Shopify rolled out Magic in 2023, a suite of generative AI tools that write product descriptions, generate product images, answer customer questions, and draft email campaigns in seconds instead of hours. Amazon deployed generative AI review summaries so customers can skim 10,000 reviews in one sentence. ChatGPT launched browse-and-buy. Google and Bing integrated shopping into generative search results. At the same time, augmented reality has matured from novelty into a measurable conversion tool: Warby Parker's virtual try-on, IKEA Place's furniture preview, and Sephora's Virtual Artist all report meaningful lift. Looking further out, voice commerce via Alexa and conversational agents threaten to disintermediate brand websites entirely. This closing session synthesizes the course themes into a forward-looking framework: what will remain scarce and valuable in an AI-saturated commerce landscape, what the durable sources of advantage are, and how to build a business that is not immediately commoditized by the next wave of technology.
key_concepts: Generative AI in e-commerce: description generation (Shopify Magic, Jasper), image generation, personalized email, chatbots (Intercom Fin, Zendesk AI)
AI search and discovery: semantic search, conversational interfaces, visual search (upload image to find similar)
AI review summarization: Amazon and Google LLMs synthesize review themes into one-sentence summaries
Augmented reality try-on: Warby Parker glasses, Sephora makeup, IKEA furniture, Wayfair home goods - conversion lift and return reduction
Voice commerce: Alexa, Google Assistant, Siri; limited beyond reorder because discovery is hard in audio-only
Conversational commerce: AI agents shopping on user's behalf, negotiating, compiling baskets across merchants
Connected device commerce: smart fridges, Dash buttons (discontinued 2019), IoT replenishment
Live AI agents: Instacart, DoorDash, Amazon testing agents that price-compare, substitute, optimize delivery
Sustainability pressure: consumers under 35 demand low-carbon shipping, recycled packaging, ethical sourcing (Patagonia, Reformation, Allbirds)
Privacy-first commerce: end of third-party cookies, Apple Mail privacy, GDPR enforcement shift value toward first-party data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through live AI examples: Shopify Magic auto-generating a product description in 5 seconds, Amazon's AI review summary compressing 5,000 reviews to one paragraph, Warby Parker virtual try-on using webcam, Perplexity running a shopping research query. Then synthesize: durable sources of advantage are (a) trust and brand, (b) proprietary data, (c) operational excellence, (d) community, (e) differentiated physical infrastructure. Everything else is compressible by AI and competition.
setup_requirements: Access to ChatGPT, Perplexity, or Claude for shopping demos
Warby Parker app for virtual try-on demo
Amazon product listing for AI review summary demo
Course synthesis framework handout

review.value: If a product description, email subject, or basic chatbot response can be generated in seconds by AI, these are no longer sources of advantage. Brands must compete on judgment, taste, brand voice, community, and operational excellence
Virtual try-on solves a real problem for glasses, shoes, cosmetics, and furniture where fit is hard to judge online. In categories without fit anxiety, AR is a novelty
Ten years after Alexa launched, voice is still used mainly for reorder and simple queries. The cognitive limits of audio-only browsing are structural. Conversational AI via screen is more promising

The e-commerce brand that survives 2030 has at least three of: trusted brand, proprietary first-party data, community/creator network, operational advantages, ethical sourcing. Paid ads and cheap manufacturing alone is extreme commoditization risk

questions.question: A new D2C sock brand is choosing between Shopify Basic ($39/month + 2.9% + $0.30) and WooCommerce (free plugin + $25/month hosting + 2.9% + $0.30 via Stripe). They expect 1,000 orders/month at $30 AOV. (a) Calculate total monthly cost under each option. (b) Explain two non-cost factors the brand should consider.
questions.answer:
(a) Monthly revenue: 1,000 * $30 = $30,000. Payment processing: 2.9% * $30,000 + $0.30 * 1,000 = $870 + $300 = $1,170 (identical in both options).

Shopify Basic: Platform $39 + Payment processing $1,170 = $1,209/month.
WooCommerce: Hosting $25 + Payment $1,170 + Developer maintenance $200 + Paid plugins $60 = $1,455/month.

Raw headline favors WooCommerce by $14 if ignoring developer time, but once maintenance is included, Shopify is typically cheaper for a non-technical founder.

(b) Non-cost factors: (1) Time-to-launch and operational simplicity: Shopify can be live in a day; WooCommerce requires technical setup and ongoing updates. A founder's time is the scarcest resource at this scale. (2) Ecosystem and integrations: Shopify has 13,000+ apps with one-click install; WooCommerce requires integration work. (3) Scalability: Shopify Plus exists as upgrade path at $2,000/month; WooCommerce needs more engineering attention as traffic grows.

rubric.key: Correct total cost calculation for both options
Recognition that payment processing is identical and not the deciding factor
Two substantive non-cost factors with clear reasoning

questions.question: A merchant does $1M/month in payments across 20,000 transactions at 2.9% + $0.30. The processor offers 'interchange plus 0.3% + $0.10' pricing where interchange averages 1.80% on their mix. Calculate current cost, new cost, and annual savings.
questions.answer:
Current cost:
Percentage: 2.9% * $1,000,000 = $29,000
Per-transaction: $0.30 * 20,000 = $6,000
Total: $35,000/month.

New interchange-plus cost:
Interchange pass-through: 1.80% * $1,000,000 = $18,000
Processor markup: 0.3% * $1,000,000 = $3,000
Per-transaction: $0.10 * 20,000 = $2,000
Total: $23,000/month.

Monthly savings: $35,000 - $23,000 = $12,000.
Annual savings: $144,000.

At $1M/month volume, the merchant is large enough for interchange-plus pricing, which strips out the processor's hidden fixed margin in favor of transparent pass-through plus explicit markup. This is why merchants above ~$250K/month in processing volume negotiate away from flat 2.9%.

rubric.key: Correct calculation of current total processing cost
Correct calculation of interchange-plus total cost
Correct monthly and annual savings with explanation

questions.question: Explain how Amazon's flywheel (selection -> customer experience -> traffic -> sellers -> selection) creates a network effect that is hard to dislodge. Then explain why the same flywheel has NOT given Amazon dominance in China or Brazil.
questions.answer:
Amazon flywheel: self-reinforcing loop where lower prices and better selection attract customers, which attracts sellers wanting access to demand, which increases selection, which attracts more customers. Each turn increases barriers to entry because competitors must match Amazon on all five dimensions simultaneously. After 25+ years, Amazon has largest selection, most reviews, fastest Prime shipping, and trust built over millions of transactions. New entrants lack all of these.

Why it failed in China: (1) Timing - Alibaba (1999) and JD.com (2004) built earlier flywheels in a market Amazon entered late.
