(2) Localization gap - Amazon never built Alipay/WeChat Pay integration, Tmall's dense layout, or Alibaba-scale logistics. (3) Regulatory environment favored domestic players. (4) Cultural fit - Taobao's live shopping, discounting, and seller-chat did not match Amazon's clean U.S. UX. Amazon closed its Chinese domestic marketplace in 2019.

Why it struggled in Brazil: (1) Mercado Libre's logistics network was locally optimized and hard to replicate given Brazil's geography. (2) Payment integration - Mercado Pago was dominant; Amazon had to compete against a better-integrated local wallet. (3) Installment culture - Brazilians expect parcelado (6-12 interest-free installments); Mercado Libre integrated this years before Amazon. (4) Tax complexity - Brazil's regime is famously complex and local players had deeper knowledge.

Lesson: the flywheel is not magic - it requires local market execution. Where Amazon did not execute on local payment, logistics, and UX, local players maintained advantages despite Amazon's global scale.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of the self-reinforcing flywheel mechanics
Identifies timing and localization as reasons for China failure
Identifies payment and logistics as reasons for Brazil challenge

questions.question: A D2C candle brand has: AOV $45, COGS $16, payment 3% + $0.30, 3PL pick/pack $1.50, packaging $2, shipping $8, return allowance 4%, CAC blended $22. (a) Calculate net profit per order. (b) What CAC target is required for break-even with 15% net margin on order 1? (c) Suggest two realistic ways to hit that CAC target.
questions.answer:
(a) Net profit per order:
Revenue $45.00
- COGS $16.00
- Payment (3% * $45 + $0.30) $1.65
- Pick/pack $1.50
- Packaging $2.00
- Shipping $8.00
- Returns (4% * $45) $1.80
- CAC $22.00
Total costs: $52.95
Net: -$7.95 (loss of $7.95/order).

(b) Target CAC for 15% margin:
15% * $45 = $6.75 target net profit.
Non-CAC costs: $16 + $1.65 + $1.50 + $2 + $8 + $1.80 = $30.95.
Max CAC: $45 - $30.95 - $6.75 = $7.30.
Merchant must reduce CAC from $22 to $7.30 (67% decrease).

(c) Paths to lower CAC:
(1) Shift channel mix toward organic (SEO, earned social, email retargeting) which typically costs 10-30% of paid acquisition per customer.
(2) Raise AOV to reduce CAC as percentage of revenue - a 3-candle bundle at $90 means $22 CAC becomes 24% of revenue instead of 49%, dramatically improving contribution margin.
(3) Referral program: $10 credit for referrer and referee can drive 15-20% of new customers at effective CAC under $5.
(4) Conversion rate optimization: cutting CAC in half via doubled conversion has the same effect as reducing ad spend. CRO on mobile checkout could plausibly reduce CAC 20-30%.

rubric.key: Correct calculation of per-order loss with all costs
Correct target CAC calculation working backward from 15% margin
Two realistic, well-reasoned paths to lower CAC

questions.question: Explain the mobile-desktop conversion gap: why do mobile visitors convert at roughly half the rate of desktop visitors, and what are three specific interventions that measurably close the gap?
questions.answer:
Mobile-desktop conversion gap (mobile ~2-3% vs desktop ~5%):

Causes: (1) Page speed - mobile devices on cellular have higher latency and less CPU; every 100ms of load time reduces conversion ~1%. (2) Form friction - typing card and address info on a small keyboard is tedious. (3) Screen real estate - product images, comparison, reviews are harder to scan; customers often research on mobile, buy on desktop. (4) Trust and security perception - some users reluctant to enter cards on public mobile. (5) Interruption - mobile sessions are frequently interrupted (notifications, tasks).

Three interventions:

(1) Mobile wallets (Apple Pay, Google Pay, Shop Pay): collapse checkout from 20+ fields to a biometric tap. Shopify reports Shop Pay converts 1.72x better than guest checkout on mobile - closes a large portion of the gap immediately.

(2) Core Web Vitals optimization: target LCP under 2.5s, INP under 200ms, CLS under 0.1. Using CDN, WebP images, deferred JavaScript, fast theme can reduce LCP from 5s to 2s, producing 5-15% conversion lift.

(3) Simplified mobile checkout: single-column forms, autofill-friendly input types (type='tel', type='email'), large tap targets, sticky CTA, guest checkout, minimal fields. Removing optional fields and using address autocomplete (Google Places API) can lift conversion 2-5%.

rubric.key: Correctly explains at least three causes of the gap
Three concrete interventions with measurable impact rationale

questions.question: A subscription razor brand has: monthly ARPU $20, COGS $7, CAC $45. At 5% monthly churn, calculate LTV and LTV:CAC. Then calculate what happens if they reduce churn to 3% through better onboarding.
questions.answer:
LTV formula: (ARPU x gross margin %) / monthly churn rate

Gross profit per subscriber per month: $20 - $7 = $13.
Gross margin: 65%.

At 5% monthly churn:
LTV = $13 / 0.05 = $260
LTV:CAC = $260 / $45 = 5.78:1 (healthy)
CAC payback = $45 / $13 = 3.5 months
Avg lifespan = 1 / 0.05 = 20 months.

At 3% monthly churn:
LTV = $13 / 0.03 = $433.33
LTV:CAC = $433.33 / $45 = 9.63:1 (exceptional)
CAC payback = 3.5 months (unchanged)
Avg lifespan = 33.3 months.

A 2-percentage-point churn reduction (40% relative improvement) produces a 67% increase in LTV ($260 -> $433). This is why subscription brands invest heavily in retention work - churn reductions compound into dramatic LTV improvements. Payback is unchanged because it depends on acquisition cost and monthly gross profit, not total lifespan. But the marginal ROI of each customer is much higher: $173 more gross profit per customer.

rubric.key: Correct LTV and LTV:CAC for 5% churn
Correct LTV and LTV:CAC for 3% churn with 67% LTV increase identified
Correct interpretation of why churn compounds and payback is unchanged

quizzes.topic: Conversion Optimization, Personalization, Trust, and Security

questions.question: A Shopify store currently requires customers to create an account before checkout. Baymard research suggests 24% of potential customers abandon when forced to register. If the store has 100,000 monthly checkout starts, 35% completion rate, and $75 AOV: (a) estimate the annual revenue lost to forced registration, and (b) design an A/B test to measure actual impact before rolling out guest checkout.
questions.answer:
(a) Annual revenue loss estimate:

Current: 100,000 starts * 35% = 35,000 orders/month * $75 = $2,625,000/month.

Recoverable via guest checkout: not all 24% are recoverable - some would abandon for other reasons. Realistic estimate: +10 percentage points completion.
New: 100,000 * 45% = 45,000 orders * $75 = $3,375,000/month.
Monthly lift: $750,000.
Annual lift: $9,000,000.

Aggressive estimate (+15 pp): $13.5M/year.

Lost annual revenue: $9M-$13.5M.

(b) A/B test design:

Hypothesis: 'Enabling guest checkout will increase checkout completion from 35% to at least 40%.'

Variants: Control (forced registration) vs Treatment (guest checkout with optional post-purchase account creation).

Primary metric: completion rate.
Secondary metrics: end-to-end order completion, AOV, return rate, 90-day repeat rate.

Sample size: baseline 35%, MDE 5pp, alpha 0.05, power 80%.
n = 2 * ((1.96 + 0.84) / 0.05)^2 * 0.375 * 0.625 ~ 1,467 per arm theoretically; 3,000-5,000 per arm for reliability. At 100K monthly starts, achievable in 2-3 days; run for 14 days minimum to capture day-of-week patterns.

Implementation: randomize at session level; exclude returning registered users from randomization; analyze at 14 days; confirm p < 0.05 before rollout.

rubric.key: Correct calculation framework for annual revenue impact
Clear A/B test hypothesis with primary and secondary metrics
Correct sample size reasoning and duration recommendation

questions.question: Explain Amazon's item-to-item collaborative filtering: why was the shift from user-to-user to item-to-item a breakthrough for scale, and how does the algorithm produce 'customers who bought this also bought' recommendations?
questions.answer:
User-to-user approach (pre-Amazon): for a user, find other users with similar purchase history (cosine similarity on purchase vectors), recommend items those similar users bought. Problem: with millions of users, pairwise similarity is O(n^2) in users and changes every purchase. At Amazon's 2003 scale, this was computationally intractable in real time.

Item-to-item insight (Linden, Smith, York, 2003): instead of comparing users, compare items. For each pair (i,j), precompute similarity based on how often they appear together in purchase histories. Key insight: the number of items is smaller than users, and item similarities are much more stable than user preferences.

Algorithm:
1. Build user-item matrix where rows are users, columns are items.
2. For each pair (i,j), compute similarity sim(i,j) = (i . j) / (|i| * |j|) (cosine similarity on the columns, essentially count of users who bought both normalized).
3. Precompute a lookup table: for each item, store top N most similar items (N=50-100).
4. At query time, when user views item i, look up top N similar items, filter out items user already owns, rank by score, return top 5-10.

Why this scales:
- Similarity computation is O(items^2) done offline in batch.
- Real-time recommendation is O(1) lookup.
- New customers cost nothing - system already knows item similarities.
- Updates can be incremental.

Amazon deployed this in early 2000s and still uses variants today. It is one reason Amazon recommendations feel instantaneous and consistently relevant, and why ~35% of Amazon revenue is reportedly attributable to recommendations.

rubric.key: Correctly explains scalability problem with user-to-user
Clear explanation of why item similarities are more stable
Correct algorithm description with similarity and precomputation

questions.question: A merchant uses Stripe for payments, which tokenizes card data. Does the merchant still need PCI-DSS compliance? Which SAQ level applies and what does that mean in practice? Explain 'scope reduction.'
questions.answer:
Yes, the merchant still needs PCI-DSS compliance; compliance is not eliminated by using Stripe. But SCOPE is dramatically reduced - the principle of scope reduction.

Applicable level: SAQ A.

SAQ A applies to merchants who fully outsource card data handling to a PCI-DSS validated third party (Stripe, Shopify Payments, Adyen) and never store, process, or transmit raw card data themselves. The payment form must be hosted or iframed from the processor.

SAQ A in practice:
- Short self-assessment questionnaire (~22 yes/no questions vs 300+ for SAQ D).
- Covers: TLS on payment pages, written security policy, monitoring for unauthorized payment page changes.
- Submitted annually, kept on file; acquirers may require attestation.
- No quarterly ASV scans beyond payment pages.
- No QSA on-site audits.

Scope reduction principle:
PCI scope is any system that stores, processes, or transmits cardholder data plus connected systems. If a merchant's server touches raw card numbers, that server and all connected systems are in scope with all 300+ controls. By using tokenization (customer types card directly into Stripe iframe, Stripe returns token, merchant stores only token), the merchant never has raw card data on its servers. The token is useless to attackers - it is merchant-specific and cannot charge any other merchant.

This is the primary modern PCI strategy: a single design choice collapses compliance from hundreds of thousands in audit effort to a short attestation. Stripe, Shopify, and Adyen are themselves PCI-DSS Level 1 certified.

Remaining obligations: TLS on payment pages; never store/log raw card data; keep payment integration current (latest Stripe.js); monitor payment pages for Magecart-style skimmer injection; maintain basic security policy.

rubric.key: Correctly states PCI is still required but scope reduced
Correctly identifies SAQ A and explains practical meaning
Clear explanation of scope reduction via tokenization

quizzes.topic: Mobile, Subscription, Social, and International Commerce

questions.question: A Shopify store sees these Core Web Vitals on mobile: LCP 4.2s, INP 320ms, CLS 0.18. Explain what each metric measures, why these are problematic, and recommend one specific fix for each.
questions.answer:
LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): time from navigation to when the largest element (hero image/heading) becomes visible. Target under 2.5s. 4.2s is POOR (Google threshold >4s).
Problem: bounce rate rises sharply above 3s; Google uses LCP as ranking signal.
Fix: LCP element is likely hero image. (1) Preload: <link rel='preload' as='image' href='hero.webp'>. (2) Serve WebP/AVIF instead of JPEG to cut size 30-50%. (3) Use Shopify image CDN with exact-size variants. (4) Remove render-blocking CSS/fonts above hero. Should bring LCP from 4.2s to under 2.5s.

INP (Interaction to Next Paint): responsiveness metric (replaced FID in 2024). Measures worst-case time from user interaction to next rendered frame. Target under 200ms. 320ms is NEEDS IMPROVEMENT (poor >500ms).
Problem: every tap feels sluggish; users lose confidence. Third-party scripts are usual culprit.
Fix: Audit JavaScript main-thread work with Chrome DevTools Performance tab. (1) Lazy-load third-party scripts (chat, heatmaps) with async/defer and initialization after user interaction. (2) Reduce Shopify apps that inject JS - each adds 50-200ms. (3) Break up long tasks (any main-thread work >50ms) using requestIdleCallback.

CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): sum of unexpected layout shifts. Target under 0.1. 0.18 is NEEDS IMPROVEMENT (0.1-0.25).
Problem: page 'jumps' as images/banners load, leading to misclicks and frustration.
Fix: (1) Add explicit width/height attributes to every image so browser reserves space before load. (2) Reserve space for announcement banners/popups with fixed-height placeholder. (3) Use font-display: optional or preload fonts. (4) Avoid injecting content above existing content - append below.

Combined expected outcome: after these fixes, all three metrics in Google 'Good' range with measurable conversion lift typically 5-15%.

rubric.key: Correct definition and threshold for each metric
Correct identification of why each number is problematic
Specific, actionable fix for each with correct technical reasoning

questions.question: Compare the Shein and Temu business models. What are the structural advantages that make their U.S. prices so low, and what are the regulatory, ethical, and sustainability risks that threaten their long-term position?
questions.answer:
Structural advantages:

1. Manufacturer-direct supply chain: Shein owns/controls thousands of Guangzhou factories. Temu (Pinduoduo) links directly to Chinese manufacturers. Cutting distributors, importers, and retailers saves 4-6x of consumer price.

2. Small-batch test-and-scale: Shein produces 100-200 units per style, observes real-time demand via app clicks, scales winners. Eliminates inventory risk. Design-to-shelf is 7 days vs 6-12 months for Zara, Gap, H&M.

3. De minimis tariff loophole: Section 321 allows packages under $800 to enter U.S. duty-free. Shein/Temu ship individual orders directly from China, bypassing tariffs (including 25% Section 301 on Chinese goods) that U.S. retailers pay on bulk imports. 20-30% price advantage.

4. TikTok-native distribution: massive influencer networks generate cheap billions of views, bypassing Meta/Google cost inflation.

5. Ultra-high SKU velocity: Shein adds 6,000-10,000 new styles/day. Customers return for novelty, reducing repeat CAC.

6. Labor cost differential: Chinese garment labor is fraction of U.S./European; enforcement of minimum wage and conditions varies.

Risks:

1. Regulatory closure of de minimis: bipartisan legislation in U.S. Congress has proposed eliminating the $800 threshold for non-market economies. Would lose 20-30% cost advantage overnight.

2. Forced labor: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA, 2021) creates rebuttable presumption that Xinjiang goods are produced with forced labor. Shein has faced repeated allegations; Customs has stopped shipments for UFLPA review.

3. IP infringement: dozens of independent designers have sued Shein for copying. Courts inconsistent but reputational/legal exposure grows.

4. Environmental: fast fashion is ~10% of global carbon emissions; Shein's volume is the largest contributor. EU Green Deal and French penalties are rising.

5. Platform risk: dependent on TikTok for U.S. CAC. A TikTok ban would disrupt marketing model.

6. Quality perception: documented sizing inconsistency, chemical findings, returns difficulty erode brand reputation.

7. Geopolitical tension: U.S.-China trade and data security concerns create ongoing uncertainty.

Bottom line: advantages are real and structural, but each is also a regulatory/reputational vulnerability. Long-term position depends on whether they pivot (onshore production, improve sourcing, diversify distribution) before regulation catches up.

rubric.key: Identifies at least 4 structural advantages with reasoning
Identifies de minimis loophole and its specific impact
Identifies at least 3 substantial risks

questions.question: A U.S. D2C skincare brand wants to launch in Germany. Walk through the five most important localization decisions they must make before going live, with specific recommendations for each.
questions.answer:
1. VAT registration:
Germany has 19% standard VAT (7% reduced). Cross-border sellers must register once EU sales exceed 10,000 Euro under OSS scheme.
Recommendation: register via One-Stop Shop in one EU country (Ireland or Netherlands for English-language ease), report German VAT through OSS. Use Avalara or Taxdoo to automate. Do NOT launch without this; back-tax penalties can exceed the tax itself.

2. Payment method localization:
German consumers do NOT primarily use credit cards. Dominant: SEPA direct debit (Lastschrift), Klarna (pay-later and pay-now invoice), PayPal, bank transfer. Credit cards are less than 20% of online payments.
Recommendation: enable PayPal as primary, add Klarna (including Kauf auf Rechnung - pay after receiving product - a uniquely German preference), enable SEPA, keep credit cards secondary.

3. Shipping and returns:
German consumers expect 2-3 day shipping and strong right-of-return. EU distance selling gives 14 days minimum to return. DHL dominates; Packstation pickup is popular.
Recommendation: partner with a German 3PL (ShipBob EU, Byrd, Huboo) to avoid shipping from the U.S. Offer free returns via DHL with printable labels. Display 14-day return period prominently.

4. Language and content:
German consumers expect German-language sites, marketing, and support. English-only sees materially lower conversion. Legal notices (Impressum) required under German law - business address, contact, VAT ID in prominent footer.
Recommendation: professional German translation (not automated). Include Impressum page. Provide customer service in German via email; consider German-language chatbot or phone.

5. GDPR and data handling:
Germany is one of the strictest enforcement jurisdictions. Cookie banners must be truly opt-in, analytics must be GDPR-compliant (GA4 IP anonymization or Plausible), DSARs handled within 30 days.
Recommendation: implement compliant cookie banner (Cookiebot, OneTrust, Usercentrics), review DPAs with every vendor, post German-language privacy policy reviewed by GDPR lawyer. Expect much lower analytics coverage (30-50% visitors rejecting tracking).

Additional: Euros with tax-inclusive display; German business hours customer service; vacation patterns (August); packaging recycling EPR registration via Licensero.

Bottom line: launching in Germany is a 6-month operational project covering tax, payment, logistics, content, and compliance, not a translation project.

rubric.key: Five substantive localization decisions correctly identified
Specific recommendations with real tools, laws, payment methods
Understanding that localization is operational not translation

quizzes.topic: Omnichannel, Marketplace Dynamics, and Future of E-commerce

questions.question: A third-party Amazon seller does $2M/year in sales with 50% COGS. Amazon's total take-rate (referral + FBA + advertising + returns) averages 42% of sale price. The founder is considering adding a Shopify store. (a) Calculate current Amazon-only net profit. (b) If Shopify captures 15% of Amazon volume at 25% blended cost, estimate combined profit. (c) Discuss strategic reasons to diversify even if economics are neutral.
questions.answer:
(a) Amazon-only net profit:
Revenue $2,000,000
COGS (50%) $1,000,000
Amazon fees (42%) $840,000
Net: $2,000,000 - $1,000,000 - $840,000 = $160,000 (8% net margin)

(b) Combined with Shopify:
Shopify volume: 15% * $2M = $300,000 net addition.
Shopify cost (25% blended): Shopify/Stripe processing ~3%, CAC ~15%, 3PL ~5%, apps/misc ~2% = 25% total.
Shopify economics:
Revenue $300,000
COGS (50%) $150,000
Shopify costs (25%) $75,000
Net from Shopify: $75,000 (25% net margin)

Combined:
Amazon $160,000 + Shopify $75,000 = $235,000 (46% increase over Amazon alone)

Additionally, Shopify yields direct customer relationships (email list, repeat, brand) that Amazon does not allow.

(c) Strategic reasons beyond economics:
1. Platform risk mitigation: Amazon can suspend for real or alleged violations, losing 100% overnight. Shopify is survivability insurance.
2. Algorithmic dependence: Amazon Buy Box and search can change without warning, dropping sales 30-50% in a week. Diversified sellers absorb this better.
3. Private label competition: if product becomes popular, Amazon Basics may clone it. Owning a brand on Shopify with direct customer list partially insulates.
4. Customer data ownership: Amazon does not share customer emails. Shopify builds an owned list worth 3-5x more in LTV.
5. Brand building: Shopify lets the seller tell a story, run a blog, build community. Amazon is a price-per-unit commodity environment.
6. Exit value: investors value diversified-channel businesses at higher multiples. Diversified $2M brand might sell at 3-4x EBITDA; Amazon-only at 1.5-2.5x due to concentration discount.
7. Wholesale expansion: Shopify site serves as reference for wholesale buyers; Amazon listing does not.

Conclusion: even if economics were neutral, risk and optionality justify diversification. The fact that economics are materially better ($75K gain) makes this an unambiguous call.

rubric.key: Correct Amazon-only profit calculation
Correct combined profit with Shopify addition
Three or more substantial strategic reasons beyond economics

questions.question: Walmart's omnichannel transformation 2016-2023 took its e-commerce from $14B to $80B+. Identify three specific moves Walmart made that drove this and explain why they worked.
questions.answer:
Three specific moves:

1. Jet.com acquisition (2016, $3.3B) and Marc Lore leadership:
Walmart acquired Jet.com primarily for founder Marc Lore and a Silicon Valley-style team, not the Jet brand (ultimately shut down). Lore was given broad authority to rebuild Walmart's e-commerce. Under his leadership, Walmart acquired ModCloth, Bonobos, Shoebuy for digital talent, relaunched Walmart.com focused on grocery and convenience, and built foundations for online grocery pickup.
Why it worked: Walmart needed not just software but culture change - from brick-and-mortar to tech-first omnichannel operator. Importing leadership and talent jumpstarted what a pure internal effort could not match at speed.

2. Free 2-day shipping and pickup/delivery investment:
In 2017, Walmart launched free 2-day shipping on $35+ orders with no membership fee - direct attack on Prime. Simultaneously invested heavily in online grocery pickup, using 4,700 stores as fulfillment nodes. By 2023, curbside pickup at 4,500+ stores, same-day delivery in many markets. Walmart+ membership ($12.95/month) launched 2020 with grocery delivery, fuel discounts, Paramount+ bundled.
Why it worked: Walmart's core advantage is proximity - average American within 10 miles of a Walmart. Using stores as mini-fulfillment eliminates the last-mile problem Amazon had to solve with expensive warehouses. Grocery is frequent-purchase; once customers shop groceries at Walmart.com, they are exposed to general merchandise.

3. Marketplace launch and expansion:
Walmart.com Marketplace launched 2009 but was sleepy for years. Under Lore, aggressively opened 2019+, grew from tens of thousands to 150,000+ sellers by 2023, approaching 500 million SKUs. Became credible Amazon alternative for 3P sellers.
Why it worked: selection breadth needed to compete with Amazon's 350 million SKUs. Walmart could not stock every SKU directly but enabled 3P to do so. Marketplace also brought advertising revenue (Walmart Connect, launched 2021, reached $2.7B in 2022), creating high-margin business offsetting retail operating margins.

Why these together produced $80B:
- Lore and team provided capability
- Grocery pickup built habit
- Free 2-day shipping removed Prime-or-wait tradeoff
- Marketplace provided selection
- Walmart+ captured loyalty
- Physical stores amortized fulfillment across online and offline

Macy's and Kohl's lacked the store footprint, technical talent, and willingness to invest at this scale over a decade. Walmart's scale and multi-year commitment made it possible.

rubric.key: Three substantive strategic moves correctly identified
Clear explanation of why each worked in context
Recognition that physical footprint was key leverage

questions.question: Synthesize the course: what are the three most durable sources of competitive advantage for an e-commerce business in 2030, and which common D2C tactics are most at risk of commoditization by AI and platform changes?
questions.answer:
Three durable sources of advantage in 2030:

1. Trust and brand equity through consistent execution:
Brand is the sum of customer expectations about how a business behaves under uncertainty. Will returns be honored? Is quality consistent? Is data safe? Brands earn this over years; AI cannot shortcut the track record. Warby Parker, Patagonia, Apple have brand moats AI cannot erode because brand is judgment of reliability, not information.

2. Proprietary data and first-party relationships:
With third-party cookies gone, iOS tracking restricted, and AI compressing value of public signals, the only durable data advantage is what YOU collect from YOUR customers. Direct email list, loyalty program, subscription, support interactions generate unique signals. Amazon's recommendation engine is only as good as Amazon's purchase data. A small brand with 100,000 engaged email subscribers has disproportionate value vs a brand with 1M followers on a platform it does not control.

3. Operational and physical infrastructure:
Logistics, fulfillment networks, supply-chain depth, in-house manufacturing, store footprints cannot be replicated by AI. Amazon's 1,500 fulfillment centers, Walmart's 4,700 stores, Zara's 7-day cycle, Shein's Guangzhou network are physical assets built over decades and billions of dollars. AI improves operations at the margin but cannot recreate physical plant.

Tactics at risk of commoditization:

1. Product description writing and basic SEO content: Shopify Magic, ChatGPT, Jasper generate serviceable copy in seconds. Brands differentiated on 'better copy' are losing that edge.

2. Generic email marketing: AI can write subject lines, segment audiences, send on-brand copy. Brands relying on marketing talent for basic email will find that talent replaceable.

3. Chatbot tier-1 customer service: Intercom Fin, Zendesk AI, custom GPT agents handle 60-80% of queries at near-zero cost. Brands that competed on responsive service via underpaid support teams will see that moat disappear.

4. Paid social as sole acquisition engine: Meta/Google auctions are more efficient but more expensive as bidders crowd in. Brands dependent on paid social for 70%+ of customers face rising CAC indefinitely.

5. Template-based design: Shopify themes, Webflow components, AI design tools produce sites that look similar. Visual differentiation now requires dedicated creative direction.

6. Commodity product dominance (generic electronics, basic apparel): Amazon Basics and Walmart private label can replicate any product in 90 days. Only defense is brand, not product itself.

Strategic implication: the e-commerce business surviving to 2030 looks less like a 2018 D2C brand chasing paid social CAC and more like a traditional brand in new clothing: durable product, genuine community, first-party relationships, operational excellence, and a category position that cannot be trivially copied.

rubric.key: Three substantive sources of durable advantage with reasoning
Four or more tactics at risk with reasoning
Strategic synthesis connecting course themes

major_assignments.title: E-commerce Business Analysis and Strategic Audit
major_assignments.description: Select a real, publicly observable e-commerce business (D2C brand, marketplace seller, or digital-first retailer). Build a complete strategic audit covering business model, competitive positioning, technology stack, unit economics inferred from public data, customer acquisition, and risks. The goal is to demonstrate the analytical skills needed to evaluate any e-commerce business you might work in, invest in, or build.
objectives: Apply business model frameworks from Laudon & Traver and Chaffey to a real company
Infer unit economics from public data including SEC filings, SimilarWeb estimates, and pricing pages
Analyze the customer acquisition channel mix and identify strengths and vulnerabilities
Produce an investment-grade strategic recommendation with specific actions

Channel mix analysis: traffic sources from SimilarWeb or SEMrush, paid vs organic split, brand vs non-brand search share

Strategic recommendation: three specific actions with expected impact and implementation timeline

objectives: Configure a working e-commerce store end-to-end including products, payments, shipping, and analytics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for in-class case discussions and store teardowns. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late except in cases of documented emergency.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts, frameworks, and cases is encouraged. All written analysis, spreadsheet models, and store configurations must be individual work. Group discussion of the midterm or final is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Using AI tools to generate substantive analysis is allowed only if explicitly disclosed with a one-paragraph statement describing what was generated and how you verified it. Undisclosed AI use is treated as plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop capable of running Chrome DevTools, a free Shopify development account via Shopify Partners, a Stripe test account, and a Google account for Sheets, Analytics, and Lighthouse. Baymard Institute free resources will be used for usability standards.
